seoTitle: How to Create a Confluence Page from Jira
metaDescription: Learn how to generate a Confluence page directly from a Jira issue, keeping documentation and development work linked together.
intro: When Jira and Confluence are connected, you can create a Confluence page straight from an issue, which keeps project documentation tied directly to the work it relates to. This guide covers generating that page and linking it back.

steps.heading: Open the Jira issue
steps.body: Navigate to the issue you want to document further in Confluence.

steps.heading: Find the Confluence link option
steps.body: Look for the option to create or link a Confluence page, typically found in the issue's more actions menu or the Confluence panel.

steps.heading: Create the page
steps.body: Choose to create a new Confluence page, which will automatically pull in relevant issue details and link back to the original ticket.

steps.heading: Add your documentation content
steps.body: Fill in the Confluence page with meeting notes, specs, or other documentation relevant to the issue.

steps.heading: Confirm the link appears on the issue
steps.body: Check that the newly created Confluence page shows up as a linked resource on the Jira issue, so anyone viewing it can find the documentation.

faq.question: Do Jira and Confluence need to be connected first?
faq.answer: Yes, this feature relies on an active link between your Jira and Confluence instances, which is typically set up automatically when both are part of the same Atlassian account.

faq.question: Can I link an existing Confluence page instead of creating a new one?
faq.answer: Yes, alongside creating a new page, Jira usually allows you to link to an already-existing Confluence page from the issue.

faq.question: Will changes to the Jira issue update the linked Confluence page?
faq.answer: No, the link connects the two, but content on each stays independent, so updates to one don't automatically edit the other.

faq.question: Who can see the linked Confluence page?
faq.answer: Visibility depends on the permissions set on the Confluence space, so make sure relevant team members have access if you want them to view the page.
